title: Collaborative Filtering: Recommend by Crowd Wisdom
concept: Collaborative filtering makes recommendations based on user-to-user similarity — finding people who share your tastes, seeing what they liked, and suggesting those items to you.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a major disadvantage of content-based filtering?

options.text: It can trap users in a 'filter bubble' by only recommending items similar to what they already liked
It requires millions of users' behavior data before it can recommend anything
It cannot run on mobile devices

exercises.explain: Exactly! Content-based systems are prone to filter bubbles since they only match attributes you have already engaged with.

cards.title: The taste twin you've never met
cards.body: Imagine another user on a music app who shares 99% of your library. You both love the same indie bands, obscure jazz tracks, and acoustic sessions. Yesterday, they discovered a new artist and loved them. Even if you've never heard of that artist, the app can confidently suggest them to you. That's collaborative filtering.

cards.title: Leveraging the crowd's patterns
cards.body: Collaborative filtering assumes that if two users agreed in the past (e.g., they both rated Movies X and Y highly), they will likely agree in the future. The system matches users with similar history, identifies items the 'tastes twins' liked that you haven't seen yet, and recommends them.

cards.title: Asking a movie club
cards.body: Instead of reading the back cover of every movie in the store, collaborative filtering is like sitting down with a group of friends who share your taste. They say: 'We all loved this new documentary!' You trust them and watch it, even though you didn't know the topic or director.

cards.title: 'Customers who bought this also bought...'
cards.body: When you buy a coffee maker on a shopping site, the system looks at all users who bought that coffee maker. It notices they also frequently bought a specific brand of coffee beans. It suggests those beans to you — even if the beans have no category tags in common with the coffee machine.

cards.title: It doesn't need to know anything about the items
cards.body: It's common to think a collaborative recommender must analyze the audio of a song or the text of a book to recommend it. In reality, collaborative filtering is blind to the content itself — it only needs the grid of user-item interactions (clicks, ratings, purchases).

cards.title: Collaborative Taste Twin Matching
cards.body: How the crowd creates recommendations for you:

left.text:
Liked: Movie 1, Movie 2
Has not seen: Movie 3

exercises.prompt: An online store recommends a camera lens to you because 80% of users who bought your camera body also purchased this lens. What type of recommendation is this?

options.text: Collaborative filtering, because it's based on other users' purchase behaviors
Content-based filtering, because it's comparing the attributes of the lens

exercises.explain: Correct! Using the purchase behaviors of the crowd to make suggestions is collaborative filtering.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Collaborative filtering can recommend a jazz album to a rock fan if other rock fans have recently started listening to that jazz album.
exercises.explain: True! Because it's based on user behavior rather than item genre tags, collaborative filtering can easily cross genres and break users out of filter bubbles.

exercises.sentence: Collaborative filtering relies on user ___ data rather than item metadata.
exercises.explain: Collaborative filtering works entirely off behavior data (likes, ratings, purchases) from the user community.

options.text: A streaming service recommends a mystery movie because users with watch histories similar to yours loved it
A streaming service recommends a mystery movie because it has the tag "mystery"
A streaming service recommends a movie with the shortest duration

exercises.explain: Correct! Recommending because similar users loved it is collaborative filtering in action.

exercises.prompt: A brand-new video platform has zero users except for a single test account, but has cataloged 10,000 video files. Can collaborative filtering be used to recommend videos on day one?

options.text: No, because collaborative filtering requires interaction data from a crowd of users to find similarities and make recommendations
Yes, because it only needs the features of the videos to run
Yes, since collaborative filtering does not use user data
No, because the algorithm requires user-item rating labels that don't exist yet

exercises.explain: Exactly! Collaborative filtering requires a community of users and their interaction history to build recommendations. With only one user, there is no crowd behavior to collaborate with.
